---
title: "Assignment 5: Nanopore base modifications"
author: Arturo Torres Ortiz
date: 03/31/2026
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    theme: cosmo
    highlight-style: github
  pdf:
    include-in-header: ../../header.tex
    callout-icon: false
    callout-appearance: default
    geometry:
      - top=30mm
      - left=20mm
---

For this assignment, we will perform differential methylation analysis using single-molecule direct DNA methylation detection from Nanopore sequencing. We will use data generated via Cas9-targeted Nanopore sequencing (nCATS) to compare the methylation profiles of a normal breast epithelial cell line (MCF-10A) against a highly aggressive triple-negative breast cancer cell line (MDA-MB-231). The files used for the assignment and their SRA accessions are summarized in the following table:

| Cell Line | Status | Library | SRA accession |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| MCF-10A | Normal breast epithelial | MinION nCATS (10 gene panel) | SRX7698109 |
| MDA-MB-231 | Breast cancer (TNBC) | MinION nCATS (10 gene panel) | SRX7698110 |

The original reference paper for this dataset:

* Gilpatrick, T., Lee, I., Graham, J.E. et al. Targeted nanopore sequencing with Cas9-guided adapter ligation. Nat Biotechnol 38, 433–438 (2020). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41587-020-0407-5

The authors developed a targeted enrichment strategy that yields extremely high sequencing coverage over specific cancer-associated loci, allowing for highly accurate, native DNA methylation profiling without the need for bisulfite conversion.

We will process the raw Nanopore signal data to identify Differentially Methylated Regions (DMRs) linked to cancer development, specifically focusing on the GSTP1 and KRT19 genes.


# Learning objectives
At the end of this week's assignment you will be able to:

1. Be familiar with single-molecule direct DNA methylation detection
2. Methylation aware asecalling from Nanopore raw signal  
3. Be able to extract methylation levels from BAM files
4. Know how to visualize methylation data
5. Be able to identify differentially methylated regions (DMRs)

# Input and outputs

The input for this assignment are the fast5 files generated by Nanopore from normal breast tissue (MCF-10A cell line) and Triple-Negative Breast Cancer (MDA-MB-231 cell line). This dataset was generated using Cas9-targeted Nanopore sequencing (nCATS), and Cas9 was used to cut and enrich specific genes before sequencing. This data is publicly available with accession number PRJNA531320.

> fast5 files:
>
> `/project2/msalomon_1816/trgn_515/5_base_mods/fast5`


You can download the data from here as well:

> Normal sample:
>
> wget https://sra-pub-src-1.s3.amazonaws.com/SRR11047866/TG_02_Mcf10a_brPanel_MinION_fast5.tgz.2

> Tumor sample:
>
> wget https://sra-pub-src-1.s3.amazonaws.com/SRR11047865/TG_03_MdaMb231_brPanel_MinION_fast5.tgz.2

# Required software
In order to complete the assignment, you can use this conda environments I prepared:

```sh
conda activate /project2/msalomon_1816/trgn_515/conda_env/trgn515
```

# Converting fast5 to pod5 format

<!-- Because these are single file fast5, run this with the ont-fast5-api software:
single_to_multi_fast5 -i fast5/MCF-10A/ -s fast5_multi/MCF-10A/ -n 4000 --recursive -t 10
single_to_multi_fast5 -i fast5/MDA-MB-231/ -s fast5_multi/MDA-MB-231/ -n 4000 --recursive -t 10
-->

The two most common ONT raw signal file formats are:

1. Fast5 is the original raw signal data from ONT. This file format has been replaced, but it's still found in many ONT runs done before 2022. For a comprehensive guide on the fast5 format: https://hasindu2008.github.io/slow5specs/fast5_demystified.pdf
2. Pod5 is the new ONT format, based on Apache arrow-columnal format. More information here: https://pod5-file-format.readthedocs.io/en/0.3.2/SPECIFICATION.html

New softare works better (and sometimes even requires!) pod5 files, so the first thing we are going to do is to transformed our fast5 files into pod5. ONT provides the software `pod5` to do exactly this:

```sh
conda activate /project2/msalomon_1816/trgn_515/conda_env/pod5

pod5 convert fast5
-t $SLURM_CPUS_PER_TASK # Multithreading
-r # Search files recursively
--output /path/to/pod5/data.pod5 # Output Pod5
/path/to/fast5/ # Folder with fast5
```

# Basecalling with Dorado

Dorado is the current ONT basecaller. It is optimized to run in GPU, especially Nvidia A100 and H100 GPU cards. To request an interactive GPU in CARC:

```sh
salloc --partition=gpu --ntasks=1 --gpus-per-task=a100:1 --mem=30G --time=1:00:00
```

You will also need to load some modules from the CUDA toolkit. To do that:

```sh
module load gcc/13.3.0
module load cuda/12.6.3
```

For more information on GPU programming in CARC, visit https://www.carc.usc.edu/user-guides/advanced-hpc-programming/gpu-programming

```sh
dorado=/project/msalomon_1385/TRGN_515/6_base_mods/dorado-0.9.1/bin/dorado
reference=/project2/biodb/genomes/Homo_sapiens/NCBI/GRCh38/Sequence/WholeGenomeFasta/genome.fa
pod5_dir=/path/to/pod5
bam_dir=/path/to/bam

$dorado basecaller -v --reference $reference --mm2-opts "-x asm10" -r sup,5mCG_5hmCG $pod5_dir/*.pod5 > $bam_dir/file.bam
```


### Task 1: Basecalling with modified bases
::: {.callout-note}
1) What is the output of the basecalling when using modified bases? **(5pts)**
2) Why do you think they have chosen this file type? **(5pts)**
3) What are the tags that represent methylation, and what do each of them mean? **(5pts)**
4) In our Dorado command, we passed the argument `-r sup,5mCG_5hmCG`. What does `sup` and `5mCG_5hmCG` mean? **(10pts)**
5) Why have we chosen `5mCG_5hmCG` a human dataset? **(10pts)**
:::


# Mapping to the reference genome

We have already aligned our reads to the reference genome, but if you hadn't done this, you would need to convert your SAM file into fastq.

```sh
samtools fastq -@ 5 -T "*" $bam_dir/M13363.sam > /path/to/fastq/M13363.fastq
```

Once we have fastq files, we can map them with minimap.

```sh
reference=/project2/biodb/genomes/Homo_sapiens/NCBI/GRCh38/Sequence/WholeGenomeFasta/genome.fa

minimap2 -ax splice \
-y
$reference /path/to/fastq/M13363.fastq | \
samtools sort -o $bam_dir/M13363.bam -
```


### Task 2: Mapping modified reads.
::: {.callout-note}
1) What does the -T flag in `samtools fastq` do and why is it important? **(5pts)**
2) What does the -y (lower case!) flag in `minimap2` do and why is it important? **(5pts)**
:::

<!-- # Extract modified base information from BAM file

We are going to use ModKit to analyze methylation data. The pileup subcommand runs through all sites or motifs and generates a probability that the site is modified.

```sh
modkit=/project/msalomon_1385/TRGN_515/6_base_mods/modkit_v0.4.4/modkit
reference=/project2/biodb/genomes/Homo_sapiens/NCBI/GRCh38/Sequence/WholeGenomeFasta/genome.fa

$modkit pileup /
--filter-threshold <float (0-1)> # General filtering
--mod-thresholds mod_code:<float (0-1)> # Modified base filtering
--ref <reference.fa> # Reference genome
<file.bam> # Input BAM file
<file.bed> # Output bed file

bgzip -k <file.bed> # Note you can also pipe the output of modkit into bgzip
tabix -p bed <file.bed.gz>


```

You can find the modified codes in Table 1. If no thresholds are specified, ModKit will calculate the appropiate threshold by taking a sample of the BAM file and calculating the 10th percentile of the probability distribution.

```{=latex}
\captionsetup[table]{
  justification = centering
}

\begin{table}[!ht]
\centering
\caption{Modified Base codes (source: https://samtools.github.io/hts-specs/SAMtags.pdf)}
\begin{tabular}{lllll}
\hline
Unmodified base    & Code & Abbreviation              & Name                      & ChEBI \\ \hline
\multirow{5}{*}{C} & m    & 5mC                       & 5-Methylcytosine          & 27551 \\
                   & h    & 5hmC                      & 5-Hydroxymethylcytosine   & 76792 \\
                   & f    & 5fC                       & 5-Formylcytosine          & 76794 \\
                   & c    & 5caC                      & 5-Carboxylcytosine        & 76793 \\
                   & C    & Ambiguity code; any C mod & Ambiguity code; any C mod & -     \\ \hline
\multirow{4}{*}{T} & g    & 5hmU                      & 5-Hydroxymethyluracil     & 16964 \\
                   & e    & 5fU                       & 5-Formyluracil            & 80961 \\
                   & b    & 5caU                      & 5-Carboxyluracil          & 17477 \\
                   & T    & Ambiguity code; any T mod & Ambiguity code; any T mod & -     \\ \hline
U                  & U    & Ambiguity code; any U mod & Ambiguity code; any U mod & -     \\ \hline
\multirow{2}{*}{A} & a    & 6mA                       & 6-Methyladenine           & 28871 \\
                   & A    & Ambiguity code; any A mod & Ambiguity code; any A mod & -     \\ \hline
\multirow{2}{*}{G} & o    & 8oxoG                     & 8-Oxoguanine              & 44605 \\
                   & G    & Ambiguity code; any G mod & Ambiguity code; any G mod & -     \\ \hline
\multirow{2}{*}{N} & n    & Xao                       & Xanthosine                & 18107 \\
                   & N    & Ambiguity code; any mod   & Ambiguity code; any mod   & -     \\ \hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}
```

::: info
**Task 4: Make a pileup of the methylation calls.**

Let ModKit calculate the filtering thresholds.

1) What are the columns of the resulting file? Visit https://github.com/nanoporetech/modkit for more information **(5pts)**
2) How many rows do you expect per genomic position? **(5pts)**
3) Can you think on some filters that may be important given the provided columns? **(5pts)**
4) What column would you use to identify different methylation patterns in each allele? How would a heterozygous methylation pattern look like? **(5pts)**
:::

# Differential methylation analysis

In differential methylation analysis, we perform a statistical test (eg: t-test) to find differences in methylation patterns between samples (eg: normal vs tumour).

We will stay within ModKit, which uses a binomial distribution to find differential methylation. In our example, we have a cancer/normal pair.


## Analyze modified bases at specific locations

If you are not interested in genome wide methylation patterns, it may be handy to subset methylation in specific regions. For human, common regions of interest would be CpG islands.

You can get a list of CpG islands from the UCSC genome browser:

```sh
wget -qO- http://hgdownload.cse.ucsc.edu/goldenpath/hg38/database/cpgIslandExt.txt.gz \
   | gunzip -c \
   | awk 'BEGIN{FS="\t"; OFS="\t"} NR>1 {print $2, $3, $4, $5}' \
   | bedtools sort -i - > cpg_islands_ucsc.bed
```


```sh
regions=/project/msalomon_1385/TRGN_515/6_base_mods/colo829/cpg_islands_ucsc.bed
reference=/project2/biodb/genomes/Homo_sapiens/NCBI/GRCh38/Sequence/WholeGenomeFasta/genome.fa

$modkit dmr pair
  -a ${norm_pileup}.gz # Bed with methylation from sample 1
  -b ${tumor_pileup}.gz # Bed with methylation from sample 2
  -o <out_file.bed> # Output to stdout if not present
  -r ${regions} # List of regions in BED format
  --ref <reference.fa> # Reference genome
  --base C # may be repeated if multiple modifications are being used
  --threads <n_threads> # Number of threads
  --log-filepath <log_file> # Path to log file
```

## Analyze modified bases at every position

If we want to get methylation patters at every position, we can skip the `--ref` option.

```sh
modkit=/project/msalomon_1385/TRGN_515/6_base_mods/modkit_v0.4.4/modkit
reference=/project2/biodb/genomes/Homo_sapiens/NCBI/GRCh38/Sequence/WholeGenomeFasta/genome.fa

$modkit dmr pair
  -a ${norm_pileup}.gz # Bed with methylation from sample 1
  -b ${tumor_pileup}.gz # Bed with methylation from sample 2
  -o <out_file.bed> # Output to stdout if not present
  --ref <reference.fa> # Reference genome
  --base C # may be repeated if multiple modifications are being used
  --threads <n_threads> # Number of threads
  --log-filepath <log_file> # Path to log file
```

## Visualize modified bases on IGV

During class, we will see how to visualize methylaytion data in IGV. Use what we learn to answer the following questions:

::: info
**Task 5: Visualize modified variants.**

Select a region from your differential methylation output (either the CpG islands or single base) and show it in IGV.

1) What are the different colors showing? **(5pts)**
2) Are there any differences between the tumor sample and the control? **(5pts)**
3) Is the tumor sample hypermethylated or hypomethylated compared to the control? Make a quick reference search to find what's the common pattern. **(5pts)**
:::

# Phasing of long reads

Phasing refers to the process of determining which alleles (genomics variants) are inherited together, identifying the parental origin of each allele and separating maternally and paternally inherited copies of each chromosome into haplotypes to get a complete picture of genetic variation. Variants within the same copy of a chromosome are in “cis”, while those from different copies are in “trans”.

Phasing can be done by looking at the heterozygous calls in a VCF that co-occur within the same read. To do that, we will use `WhatsHap`.

First, we phase the variants within the VCF file:

```sh
whatshap phase
--ignore-read-groups # Adding this because in our examples sample names in BAM and VCF do not match
-o <output.vcf> # Output VCF file
--reference=<reference.fa> # Reference genome
<input_vcf> # Input VCF file
<input_bam> # Input BAM file
```

Once we have the phased variants, we can find out which reads within a BAM file belong to each haplotype. We will add a haplotype tag to each read individually. 

```sh
whatshap haplotag
--ignore-read-groups
-o <out_bam> # Output BAM file
--reference=<reference.fa> # Reference genome
--output-threads=<n_threads> # Threads for BAM compression
<input_phased_vcf> # Input phased VCF file
<input_bam> # Input BAM file
``` -->
